content.text: Personal data leaks into logs constantly and quietly — inside prompts, tool arguments, error messages, and stack traces.
PII scrubbing in log ingestion pipelines

is the practice of detecting and removing or masking personal data as logs are ingested, before they are written to durable storage, so sensitive content never lands in the log backend in the first place.
What it is

PII (personally identifiable information) scrubbing is a transformation stage in the log pipeline that inspects each incoming log event and redacts values that match personal-data patterns — email addresses, phone numbers, credit-card numbers, national IDs, names, and free-form user content. It runs at
ingest time
, in the collector or a stream processor sitting between the application and storage, so the scrub happens once, centrally, and cannot be forgotten by an individual service.

Scrubbing combines two strategies: structural redaction (masking fields the schema already knows are sensitive) and content detection (pattern- and ML-based scanning of free-text fields for PII that leaked into places it should not be). The output is a log event that preserves operational structure — timestamps, IDs, outcomes — while replacing personal values with tokens, hashes, or masks.
Why it matters

For agentic AI, prompts and tool arguments routinely contain whatever the user typed, which can be anything: a home address, a medical detail, a password pasted by mistake. If those flow unscrubbed into a log store, the store becomes a shadow copy of personal data subject to GDPR, HIPAA, or CCPA — with all the retention, access-control, and breach-notification obligations that implies, but usually none of the controls. Scrubbing at ingest keeps the blast radius small: even if the log store is later breached or over-broadly accessed, it contains no raw personal data. It also makes right-to-erasure tractable, because there is far less personal data to find and delete.
Benefits
Personal data never reaches durable log storage, shrinking breach and compliance exposure.
Centralised at ingest, so every service is covered without per-service redaction code.
Reduces the scope of right-to-erasure and data-subject-access requests against logs.
Operational fields survive scrubbing, so debugging and aggregation still work.
A single policy change updates redaction everywhere, rather than redeploying every service.
Trade-offs and limitations
Detection is imperfect: regex misses novel formats and ML detectors have false negatives, so some PII can slip through.
Over-aggressive scrubbing destroys useful debugging context (masking an ID that was not actually personal).
Content scanning adds latency and CPU cost to the ingest path, which must keep up with log volume.
Scrubbing at ingest cannot recover data already logged unscrubbed before the rule existed.
Free-form names and addresses are the hardest to detect reliably and the most sensitive to miss.
How it works

The pipeline applies a layered detector to each event. Structural rules mask fields whose keys are known-sensitive. Pattern detectors use anchored regular expressions for well-structured PII (emails, cards with a checksum, phone numbers). A named-entity recognition (NER) model scans remaining free-text fields for person names, locations, and organisations that have no fixed format. Each match is replaced by a typed placeholder such as
<EMAIL>
or
<PERSON>

, preserving the fact that a value was present without preserving the value. Detected credit-card candidates are additionally validated with the Luhn checksum to cut false positives on random digit strings.

Scrubbing effectiveness is measured on a labelled evaluation set as recall (the fraction of true PII caught) and precision (the fraction of redactions that were genuinely PII). Because a missed leak is worse than an over-redaction, the policy is tuned to favour recall:
Architecture

attrs.alt: Log events pass through structural masking and content detection before durable storage

content.text: Raw events enter the collector, where structural masking handles known-sensitive fields and a detector chain scans free text with regex, checksum validation, and an NER model. Matches are replaced with typed placeholders, and only the scrubbed event is forwarded to storage. The original raw event is never persisted; if a raw copy is needed for a narrow purpose, it goes to a separate, tightly-controlled, short-retention buffer.
When to use it
Any agent system whose prompts, tool inputs, or errors can contain user-provided text.
Deployments under GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, or contractual data-handling obligations.
When logs are shared with third-party observability SaaS that should not receive personal data.
Less critical for logs that provably contain only synthetic or non-personal machine data.
How to implement
Inventory which fields and free-text sources can contain PII across the agent's log events.
Define structural masking for all known-sensitive field keys at the collector.
Add anchored regex detectors for structured PII and validate candidates (e.g. Luhn for cards).
Add an NER model pass for unstructured names, locations, and organisations in free-text fields.
Replace matches with typed placeholders and forward only scrubbed events to storage.
Build a labelled evaluation set and measure recall and precision; tune to favour recall.
Run the scrubber in the ingest path with back-pressure handling so it cannot be bypassed.
Examples
Real-world example

A financial-services agent lets users ask questions in natural language, so prompts routinely contain account numbers and names. The team ran scrubbing in their OTel Collector: structural masking on the prompt and tool-argument fields, regex plus Luhn for card and account numbers in error messages, and an NER pass for names in free-text tool outputs. On a labelled sample the pipeline reached 98.6% recall on structured PII and 91% on names, and the residual name misses were funnelled into a weekly review that expanded the NER training data.

When a routine security review later examined the log store, it found typed placeholders where personal data would otherwise have been, confirming that even engineers with broad log access could not read customers' account numbers. The raw-event buffer used for a narrow fraud-analysis use case had a 24-hour retention and separate access controls, keeping the sensitive path tightly bounded.
Common mistakes
Relying on regex alone, which misses unstructured PII like names and addresses entirely.
Scrubbing after storage instead of at ingest, so raw PII still lands durably first.
Letting the scrubber be optional or per-service, so one un-instrumented service leaks everything.
Tuning for precision over recall, so misses (the dangerous errors) increase to avoid over-redaction.
Forgetting stack traces and exception messages, which often embed the offending input value.
Best practices
Scrub at ingest in a shared collector so coverage is universal and cannot be forgotten.
Layer structural, regex+checksum, and NER detection; no single method catches all PII.
Measure recall and precision on a labelled set and tune to favour recall.
Replace with typed placeholders so the presence of a value is retained without the value.
Keep any raw-data path separate, short-retention, and access-controlled — never the default.
Performance and scalability

Regex and structural masking are cheap and scale linearly with event size. The NER pass is the expensive stage — a transformer NER model can process only thousands of short texts per second per core, which can bottleneck a high-volume ingest path. Mitigate by only running NER on fields that structural and regex passes did not already fully mask, batching inputs, and running the model on dedicated workers scaled horizontally behind the collector. If throughput cannot keep up, apply NER selectively to the highest-risk fields and fall back to regex elsewhere, accepting a lower recall on low-risk fields. Always design for back-pressure so a slow detector drops-with-a-counter rather than stalling log ingestion.

Security and reliability

The scrubber is a privacy control on the critical path, so its failure modes matter. A crash must not silently forward raw events unscrubbed; fail closed by dropping or quarantining events the scrubber could not process, and alert on the quarantine rate. Protect the detector configuration and any NER model in version control with review, since a bad rule change can quietly disable redaction. Periodically sample stored logs and scan them with an independent detector as a canary — if that scan finds PII, the primary scrubber has a gap. Keep the labelled evaluation set itself in a secured location, as it necessarily contains real PII examples.
Mental model

Think of ingest scrubbing like a mailroom that opens every incoming package and removes anything hazardous before it enters the building. Once a package is inside and shelved, retrieving and neutralising a hazard is expensive and error-prone; screening at the single entry point is cheap and complete. Structural masking is checking the packages you already know are dangerous by their label; regex and NER are the scanners that catch hazards hidden inside ordinary-looking parcels. The mailroom must fail closed — a scanner outage means packages wait, not that they pass through unscreened.
Managing false negatives with continuous evaluation

No detector is perfect, so a scrubbing program must treat missed PII as an expected, measurable event rather than an impossibility. Continuous evaluation runs an independent, higher-recall detector over a sample of already-stored logs on a schedule; any PII it finds is a leak the primary scrubber missed. Those findings feed two actions: an immediate targeted deletion of the leaked values, and an update to the primary detector (a new regex, an expanded NER training set) so the same class of leak is caught next time.

This closes the loop between detection quality and real outcomes. Because the canary detector runs offline and on a sample, it can afford to be slower and more aggressive than the ingest-path scrubber, catching leaks the fast path cannot while keeping the ingest path within its latency budget.
Run an independent, higher-recall canary detector over sampled stored logs on a schedule.
Treat any canary finding as a real leak: delete it and improve the primary detector.
Let the offline canary be slower and more aggressive than the latency-bound ingest scrubber.
Track missed-PII rate as a program metric; a rising trend means detectors are falling behind traffic.
Feed detector gaps back into regex rules and NER training so recall improves over time.
Keep canary findings access-controlled — they point directly at where PII leaked.
Where PII hides in agent logs

attrs.alt: Map of where PII commonly leaks in agent logs, from expected fields to surprising ones

content.text: The fields everyone remembers to scrub — prompt and tool arguments — are only the obvious sites. PII leaks most often through the paths nobody thought to scrub: an exception message that embeds the offending input, a retry payload copied verbatim, a URL with the user's query in the query string, or worst of all a metric label that pins high-cardinality personal data into a time-series database. A thorough scrubbing program maps all three tiers, not just the expected fields.
Key takeaways
Scrub PII at ingest, before durable storage, so raw personal data never lands in the log backend.
Layer structural masking, regex with checksum validation, and NER — no single method suffices.
Measure recall and precision on a labelled set and tune to favour recall, since misses are the costly error.
Replace values with typed placeholders to keep operational structure without the sensitive content.
Run scrubbing centrally so no service can bypass it, and fail closed on detector errors.
NER is the throughput bottleneck; scope it to residual free text and scale it horizontally.
